name: Crown Glass Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Crown Glass. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Crown Glass surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength providing optimal absorption in surface contaminants while minimizing glass substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate balancing cleaning efficiency and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal below glass damage threshold

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient contaminant removal with minimal thermal diffusion into glass substrate

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm laser cleaning of delicate glass substrates like Crown Glass to remove contaminants without inducing thermal stress; starting at 50 W allows controlled ablation. Max safe before damage ~200 W depending on fluence. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for industrial cleaners; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Glass' (Optics and Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contaminant removal on glass; slower speeds (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (500-2000 mm/s) for light; 500 mm/s optimal to minimize heat accumulation on low-thermal-conductivity Crown Glass. Source: Industry standards from Laser Institute of America guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for Crown Glass to balance ablation efficiency and minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but costlier applications; ns avoids excessive plasma formation on reflective glass surfaces. Source: Laser physics studies in 'Pulsed Laser Cleaning of Glass' (Applied Surface Science, 2015), recommending ns for industrial use.

repetition_rate.description: Standard range for 1064 nm cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for Crown Glass to achieve fast coverage without significant heat buildup, as higher rates (>50 kHz) increase thermal load on glass with moderate reflectivity (~4-8% at 1064 nm). Trade-off: higher freq speeds process but risks microcracks. Source: Academic literature in Journal of Applied Physics (2020) on pulse train effects in glass cleaning.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe contaminant ablation on Crown Glass; damage threshold ~10-15 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to low absorption; 2 J/cm² recommended to stay below threshold while effective for oxides/dirt removal, considering glass melting point ~500°C. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Optics Express (2017) and ISO 21254 standards for laser-induced damage.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for uniform cleaning; 100-200 μm for fine surface prep on optical Crown Glass, up to 500 μm for large-area industrial use; 200 μm balances resolution and efficiency without focusing-induced hotspots. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and SPI Lasers for 1064 nm systems.

pass_count.description: Typically 1-2 passes for light contamination on Crown Glass, up to 5 for heavy buildup; multi-pass at lower fluence preferred to avoid cumulative thermal damage. Source: Industry case studies in 'Industrial Laser Cleaning Applications' (Proceedings of SPIE, 2019) for glass substrates.

overlap_ratio.description: Overlap ensures uniform coverage without gaps or over-ablation; 70% recommended for Crown Glass to achieve even cleaning on flat surfaces while minimizing total energy input and thermal gradients. Source: Process optimization in Laser & Photonics Reviews (2016), typical 50-80% for scanned beam cleaning.
